# TCP in-flight visualizer

> An interactive browser-based simulator for **TCP behavior on a single bulk transfer**. Visualizes the sender, receiver,
> kernel buffers on both ends, the bottleneck router queue between them, and the network in between. Includes a
> programmable JavaScript API (`window.SimAgent`) so agents can configure runs, read structured results, and watch the
> same animation a human would see.

The deployed root (`index.html`) is a thin iframe wrapper. The actual page (with `window.SimAgent`) is
`tcp-inflight.html` and loads inside the iframe. Agents driving the deployed root should target the iframe's
`contentWindow`; agents loading `tcp-inflight.html` directly get `window.SimAgent` on the top window.

---

## What this is

TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is the protocol that does almost all bulk data transfer on the internet — file
uploads, HTTP requests, video streaming control planes, etc. It's reliable (retransmits lost packets), ordered, and
**congestion-controlled** (adapts its send rate to network conditions). The exact way TCP adapts — its *congestion
control algorithm* — has huge implications for real-world throughput, latency, and how a single connection behaves on
shared infrastructure.

This site simulates a single TCP transfer (one client → one server, sending e.g. a 10 MB body) over a network with a
configurable bottleneck. You can watch every byte of the request leave the sender, traverse the network, queue at the
bottleneck, and arrive at the receiver. The simulator models **TCP Cubic** (the default congestion control in Linux
since ~2008), with slow-start, Hystart, fast retransmit, fast recovery, and RTO, plus **TCP BBR** (Google's 2016
model-based controller). It also models **bottleneck queue dynamics** — packets fill the queue, queue overflow causes
drops, drops trigger retransmits — and **flow control** via finite kernel buffers.

The intended audience is anyone who wants to *see* how TCP works under specific conditions: developers debugging
transfer performance, students learning networking, agents running experiments. The visualization is the teaching tool;
the agent API makes it possible to run experiments programmatically.

## What the screen shows

**Top region** (~65% of canvas):

- **Left column — Sender**:
  - *Body grid* on top: a grid of cells, one per MSS-sized packet of the payload. Cells are bright cyan when "buffered"
    (data exists on the sender ready to go), blue when "in flight" (this packet has been sent but not yet
    acknowledged), dim when "drained" (sent and acknowledged).
  - *send-buffer bucket (wmem)* below: visualizes the kernel send buffer (`tcp_wmem`). Cells are packet-sized slots. Filled cells = bytes
    the app has written to the kernel that haven't been ACKed yet.
- **Middle column — HUD**: live numbers — goodput (KB/ms and Mbps), bound-by, request time, completion %, and a dense
  stats grid below with cwnd, in-flight bytes, queue depth, retransmits, recv window, etc.
- **Right column — Receiver**: mirror of the sender's body grid and recv-buffer bucket (rmem). Cells turn bright cyan as packets
  arrive.

**Bottom region** (~35% of canvas, full canvas width):

- **Network band**: a horizontal strip showing packets in transit. Split in two legs (pre-queue, post-queue) by the
  bottleneck router buffer in the middle. Data packets (blue dots) flow left-to-right along the top half; ACKs (green
  dots) flow right-to-left along the bottom half. Packet Y position is random (just for visual separation — TCP is a
  single byte stream physically).
- **Router buffer** (bottleneck): a vertical bucket in the middle of the band. Cells = MSS-sized slots; the bucket's
  height scales with the configured `queue` size. When a packet arrives at a full router buffer, it gets *dropped* —
  visualized as a red dot falling out the bottom of the bucket (gravity-like animation in real time).

**Sidebar** (right of canvas):

- Preset selector + scenario card (story, what to watch, expected outcome)
- Knobs (sliders): RTT, cwnd, MSS, payload, sim speed, link bw, router buffer, loss %, and the congestion-control
  options.
- Collapsible "Buffers" section: send buffer (wmem), app write rate, receive buffer (rmem), app read rate.
- Start / Pause / Reset controls.
- Glossary: clickable terms (RTT, BDP, cwnd, Hystart, fast retransmit, etc.) with definitions. The canvas components
  themselves are also click targets (body grids, wmem/rmem buckets, router queue bucket, network band), as are legend
  items, and glossary/preset prose cross-links every known term it mentions.

## Key TCP concepts you'll see in action

Anchored to what each one looks like on screen:

- **RTT** (round-trip time, in ms): how long a packet plus its ACK takes to traverse the network. Each packet takes
  RTT/2 across the band; its ACK takes another RTT/2 back. Knob: `RTT (ms)`.
- **MSS** (maximum segment size, bytes): how big each packet is (TCP's formal term for its data unit: segment). Typically ~1300–1500 bytes. Knob: `Packet size (MSS)`.
- **cwnd** (congestion window, in packets): a per-connection cap on the number of packets the sender may have
  *unacknowledged on the wire*. The goodput a single TCP flow can sustain is approximately `cwnd × MSS / RTT`. Knob:
  `cwnd (pkts)`.
- **BDP** (bandwidth-delay product): `link_bw × RTT / 8` in bytes — the "natural pipe size" of the path. Cwnd should
  reach BDP for TCP to fill the pipe. Derived from knobs.
- **slow-start**: TCP's startup phase. Cwnd starts small (typically 10 packets) and doubles every RTT until something
  stops it (loss, Hystart, or `ssthresh`). Animation: cwnd ramps up exponentially.
- **Hystart**: an algorithm that exits slow-start *before* cwnd causes a loss by detecting queueing (the simulator's
  Hystart fires when cwnd × MSS > BDP). Animation: after Hystart fires, cwnd stops doubling and grows linearly.
- **Cubic**: the modern congestion control algorithm. On loss, cwnd is cut multiplicatively (×0.7 in Cubic). It then
  regrows along a cubic curve toward the prior peak. Default since Linux 2.6.19.
- **BBR**: Google's 2016 model-based congestion control. Instead of reacting to packet loss, it continuously estimates
  two physical quantities — `BtlBw` (max delivery rate over the last ~10 RTTs) and `RTprop` (min RTT over the last
  ~10 sec). The sender paces packets at `BtlBw × pacing_gain` with cwnd capped at `BtlBw × RTprop × cwnd_gain`
  (~2× BDP). State machine: STARTUP doubles per RTT until delivery rate plateaus, DRAIN drains the queue STARTUP
  built, PROBE_BW runs an 8-phase pacing cycle `[1.25, 0.75, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1]` to keep probing for more bandwidth,
  PROBE_RTT briefly drops cwnd to 4 packets every 10 sec to re-measure RTprop. Largely ignores packet loss — designed for
  lossy or shallow-buffered paths where Cubic collapses.
- **Fast retransmit**: when 3 dup-ACKs arrive (signal that a packet is missing), the sender retransmits without waiting
  for an RTO timeout.
- **Fast recovery**: the cwnd reduction (×0.7) is mild and recovery proceeds via cubic regrowth; cwnd doesn't crash to
  the initial value.
- **RTO** (retransmission timeout): the catastrophic loss path. Cwnd resets to the initial value (e.g., 10) and
  slow-start restarts. Reserved for serious problems.
- **Router buffer (bottleneck queue)**: the buffer at the path's slowest link. Packets queue here when arrival rate >
  drain rate. If it fills, packets drop. Knob: `Router (KB)`.
- **send / receive buffers (wmem / rmem)**: the sender's and receiver's kernel TCP buffers. wmem holds bytes from the app's write call until
  ACKed (kernel needs them in case of retransmit). rmem holds arrived bytes until the receiver app reads them. The
  receiver advertises `rwnd = rmem − rmem_used` to the sender; sender's effective window is `min(cwnd, rwnd)`. Knobs in
  the "Buffers" section.

## Knobs (full reference)

All knobs are settable via `SimAgent.configure({...})` or via the sliders in the sidebar.

| Knob | API param | Range | What it does |
|---|---|---|---|
| RTT (ms) | `rtt` | 1–1000 | Round-trip time. Packets take RTT/2 across the band. |
| cwnd (pkts) | `cwnd` | 1–65536 | Hard cap on unacked segments. Used directly in `custom` cc mode; auto-managed in `cubic`/`bbr`. |
| Init cwnd (pkts) | `initialCwndSeg` | 1–65536 | Cwnd at the start of a cubic or BBR run (RFC 6928 default is 10). |
| Packet size (MSS) | `mss` | 500–9000 | Max data bytes per packet. |
| Payload (MB) | `payloadMb` | 0.1–500 | Total request size. |
| Sim speed | `simSpeed` | 0–1 | Wall-clock to sim-clock ratio (log scale). 0.02 = 50× slow-mo for watching. |
| Link bw (Mbps) | `linkBwMbps` | 1–100000 | Bottleneck rate the flow sees. |
| Router (KB) | `queueKB` | 4–65536 | Bottleneck buffer size (log-scale slider). |
| Loss (%) | `lossPct` | 0–20 | Background per-packet random loss (independent of queue overflow). |
| send buf (KB) | `wmemKB` | 4–32768 | Sender kernel send buffer (wmem). |
| recv buf (KB) | `rmemKB` | 4–32768 | Receiver kernel recv buffer (rmem). |
| app write (Mbps) | `appWriteMbps` | 0–10000 | App→wmem write rate. 0 = saturate. |
| app read (Mbps) | `appReadMbps` | 0–10000 | rmem→app drain rate. 0 = saturate. |
| Mode | `mode` | tcp \| udp | UDP disables ACKs, retransmits, flow control. |
| Handshake | `handshake` | true \| false | TCP 3-way handshake before data. When true, the first data segment leaves at exactly t = RTT (SYN out, SYN-ACK back; the final ACK rides with the first data flight). Default false (warm/reused connection). Ignored in UDP mode. |
| CC algo | `ccMode` | custom \| cubic \| bbr | "custom" keeps cwnd at the knob value; "cubic" runs RFC 8312; "bbr" runs Google's 2016 BBR. |

### Presets

Seven scenario presets, each a self-contained lesson. A preset sets every model knob plus the congestion-control mode
and initial cwnd. Apply via `SimAgent.applyPreset(name)`:

| Preset key | Scenario | The lesson |
|---|---|---|
| `healthy` | 40 ms RTT, 200 Mbps, ample buffer | Clean slow-start → Hystart → saturated link. 10 MB in ~0.7 s. |
| `cwnd-buildup` | 150 ms RTT, 300 Mbps (big BDP), IW 10 | Cold connections spend the transfer ramping cwnd. 20 MB in ~2 s. |
| `warm-start` | Same path, IW = BDP (~3852 pkts) | The ramp disappears: same 20 MB in ~0.8 s. |
| `congestion` | 60 ms RTT, 150 Mbps, 96 KB router buffer | Shallow-buffer overflow: drops, sawtooth, ~5 Mbps crawl. |
| `bufferbloat` | 30 ms RTT, 50 Mbps, 4 MB buffer, fixed cwnd 2000 | Standing queue ~450 ms dwell; nothing drops, nothing slows down. |
| `lossy` | 50 ms RTT, 100 Mbps, 2% random loss | Cubic usually crawls at ~1 Mbps (high run-to-run variance); `bbr` → ~25 Mbps consistently. |
| `slow-receiver` | 200 Mbps link, app reads at 40 Mbps, 256 KB recv buffer | rwnd collapse + zero-window stalls; flow control sets the pace. |

## Agent API: `window.SimAgent`

Loaded on the page. Use it from page-world JavaScript (Playwright's `page.evaluate`, devtools console, browser-extension
injected scripts, or similar). From within an iframe-wrapper page, access via
`document.querySelector('iframe').contentWindow.SimAgent`.

### Methods

```js
// --- configure ---
SimAgent.applyPreset(name)                   // preset key from the table above — resets
SimAgent.configure(params)                   // partial config, see knobs table for keys
SimAgent.setMode("tcp" | "udp")
SimAgent.setCcMode("custom" | "cubic" | "bbr")

// --- control ---
SimAgent.reset()
SimAgent.start()                             // begin RAF loop (visual playback)
SimAgent.pause()
SimAgent.step(ms)                            // advance N sim-ms synchronously
SimAgent.isRunning()                         // bool

// --- run-to-completion (both deterministic and visual) ---
await SimAgent.runToCompletion({ visual, maxSimMs })
  // visual: false (default) — tight loop, returns in ms wall-time, no animation
  // visual: true            — RAF loop, animates at current simSpeed, resolves on finish
  // maxSimMs: cap on sim time (default 60000)

await SimAgent.runUntil(predicate, { visual, maxSimMs })
  // predicate(state) returning bool — stop when true
  // e.g. SimAgent.runUntil(s => s.cc_phase === "cong-avoid")

// --- read ---
SimAgent.getState()                          // current state snapshot
SimAgent.getSummary()                        // computed summary of the run
SimAgent.getTrace()                          // array of 10 Hz samples
SimAgent.getEvents()                         // discrete events
SimAgent.getArtifact()                       // { run_id, version, config, samples, events, summary }

// --- replay a captured artifact, visually ---
await SimAgent.replay(artifact, { speed })

// --- meta ---
SimAgent.version                             // "1.0"
```

### Sample shape (10 Hz)

```js
{
  t_ms: 412,                       // sim time of this sample
  cwnd_seg: 1284,                  // current cwnd in segments
  cc_phase: "cong-avoid",          // "slow-start" | "cong-avoid"
  in_recovery: false,
  inflight_seg: 950,
  inflight_bytes: 1024576,
  q_depth_bytes: 384000,           // bottleneck queue depth
  q_drops_cumulative: 2,
  retransmits_cumulative: 0,
  acked_seg: 412,
  recv_seg: 489,
  goodput_KBms: 14.8,
  goodput_Mbps: 121,
  rtt_ms: 60,                      // knob at moment of sample
  link_bw_mbps: 230,
  mss_B: 1398,
  wmem_used_bytes: 1024576,
  rmem_used_bytes: 81084,
  advertised_rwnd_seg: 2950
}
```

### Event types

Emitted at most once per transition:

- `handshake_done`: the 3-way handshake completed and data may flow (only with `handshake: true`; fires at t ≈ RTT)
- `first_loss`: first time the simulation experiences any loss (queue or background)
- `hystart_exit`: cwnd crossed BDP and slow-start switched to cong-avoid
- `recovery_enter` / `recovery_exit`: fast-recovery boundaries
- `rto`: a retransmission-timeout fired (severe loss path)
- `queue_drop_burst`: ≥5 drops in one sample interval (sign of bursty overflow)
- `bbr_state_change`: BBR transitioned between states (startup / drain / probe_bw / probe_rtt). Data includes the
  current `btlBw_Mbps` and `rtprop_ms`. Only emitted in `ccMode: "bbr"`.
- `finished`: sim completed (all payload acknowledged)

### Artifact shape

`getArtifact()` returns a fully self-contained record of the run:

```js
{
  run_id: "uuid",
  version: "1.0",
  config: { /* every knob value at run time */ },
  samples: [ /* 10 Hz snapshots */ ],
  events: [ /* discrete events */ ],
  summary: {
    completed: true,
    elapsed_sim_ms: 725,
    acked_segments: 7182,
    acked_pct: 100,
    goodput_KBms: 14.46,
    goodput_Mbps: 115.7,
    cwnd_min: 10, cwnd_max: 713, cwnd_final: 713,
    queue_drops_total: 0,
    retransmits_total: 0,
    wmem_peak_bytes: 4194000,
    rmem_peak_bytes: 215292,
    first_loss_t_ms: null,
    hystart_exit_t_ms: 502.5
  }
}
```

## Examples

### Run a scenario deterministically

```js
SimAgent.applyPreset("healthy");
await SimAgent.runToCompletion({ visual: false });
SimAgent.getSummary();
// → { completed: true, elapsed_sim_ms: 725, goodput_Mbps: 115.7, ... }
```

### Sweep a knob, watch each run

```js
const results = [];
for (const queueKB of [50, 200, 500, 1500, 4500]) {
  SimAgent.applyPreset("congestion");
  SimAgent.configure({ queueKB, simSpeed: 0.3 });
  await SimAgent.runToCompletion({ visual: true });
  results.push({ queueKB, ...SimAgent.getSummary() });
}
```

### Stop on first loss

```js
SimAgent.applyPreset("congestion");
await SimAgent.runUntil(s => s.q_drops_cumulative > 0 || s.retransmits_cumulative > 0);
SimAgent.getState();
// → state at first loss
```

### Compare cubic to BBR on a lossy link

```js
const traces = {};
for (const cc of ["cubic", "bbr"]) {
  SimAgent.applyPreset("lossy");
  SimAgent.setCcMode(cc);
  await SimAgent.runToCompletion({ visual: false });
  traces[cc] = SimAgent.getArtifact();
}
```

## Driving the page from outside the browser

The agent API lives in the page's JavaScript context. To reach it from external automation:

- **Playwright / Puppeteer**: `page.evaluate(async () => { ... SimAgent.something() })` works directly because
  `page.evaluate` runs in page world. See `examples/agent-driver.js`.
- Browser automation that runs scripts in an *isolated world* (e.g. AppleScript's `execute javascript`) can't see
  `window.SimAgent` directly. Workaround: inject a `<script>` element into the page world, have it write results to
  `document.title`, then poll the title from outside.

## Fidelity notes

This is a teaching model, not a packet-accurate emulator. `link_bw` represents the *effective bottleneck rate this
flow sees* (its share of capacity after contention), not the nominal wire bandwidth. Cubic sends are ACK-clocked but
unpaced — a cwnd-worth of segments can hit the queue at line rate, which makes shallow-buffer scenarios harsher than
reality. Hystart exits slow-start at exactly BDP and switches to linear AIMD growth until the first loss anchors the
cubic curve. The RTO model is simplified (fires one RTT after send; no exponential backoff). BBR repairs losses
quickly (SACK-style: an unACKed segment with 3 ACKed segments above it is retransmitted immediately, plus dup-ACK
fast retransmit) but ignores them for rate control, as BBRv1 largely did — its window/pacing come from the
BtlBw/RTprop model only, so random loss barely dents BBR goodput while it cripples cubic. The `rto` event is not
emitted in BBR mode.

## Constraints (things you cannot do as an agent)

- Read the rendered canvas pixels — only structured state via the API.
- Modify the page DOM/CSS beyond what the agent API allows.
- Persist state across page reloads.
- Run multiple simulations in parallel from a single tab.

## Source

The architecture, in five files:

- `sim-core.js` — pure simulation model (sim state factory, step function, presets). No DOM. Usable in Node or browser.
- `sim-runner.js` — shared runner. Owns the RAF loop (browser) and the tight deterministic loop (Node). Captures 10 Hz
  samples and discrete events.
- `sim-agent-api.js` — exposes `window.SimAgent`.
- `tcp-inflight.html` — the page. Visualization, controls, hooks into the runner.
- `sim-cli.js` — CLI usage (`node sim-cli.js --preset healthy`).

Plus `examples/agent-driver.js`, a Puppeteer script that drives the page end to end.
